title: What Are My Rights as a Commercial Tenant in NSW?
metaTitle: Commercial Tenant Rights NSW: What You're Actually Protected By | Your Office Space
metaDescription: Commercial tenants in NSW have fewer statutory protections than retail tenants. Your rights mostly come from your lease. Here's what to know.
excerpt: Commercial tenants in NSW aren't covered by the Retail Leases Act. Most of your rights come from the lease itself — which is why the lease matters so much.
author: Joe Kelley
body:
## What rights does a commercial tenant have in NSW?

If you're a commercial tenant in NSW, your legal protections are more limited than you might think. The Retail Leases Act 1994 provides significant protections — but only for retail tenants. If you're in an office, warehouse, or industrial space, that Act likely doesn't apply to you. Your rights are mostly determined by your lease document, not legislation.

This is not a reason to panic. It is a reason to take your lease seriously.

> **This article is general information only. It is not legal advice. For advice specific to your situation, speak to a commercial solicitor.**

## The Retail Leases Act 1994 — who it covers and who it doesn't

The Retail Leases Act 1994 (NSW) covers most retail shops in NSW. It provides statutory protections including mandatory disclosure requirements, rent review processes, and restrictions on certain lease terms.

If your space is used for retail purposes and falls within the Act's definition of a retail shop, you have those protections.

If your space is an office, a warehouse, a factory, or a professional services suite that isn't retail, the Act generally doesn't apply. That covers a large proportion of commercial tenants in NSW.

## What rights do you still have as a commercial tenant?

Even without the Retail Leases Act, you're not without any protections. Here's what matters:

### Quiet enjoyment

Every commercial lease includes an implied (and often express) covenant of quiet enjoyment. This means the landlord cannot interfere with your use of the space during the lease term, provided you're meeting your obligations. If a landlord changes locks, cuts off utilities without cause, or repeatedly harasses you, that's a breach.

### Disclosure obligations

While the Retail Leases Act mandates disclosure documents, commercial landlords still have general obligations under contract law not to misrepresent material facts. If a landlord tells you the building's air conditioning is fully operational and it isn't, you may have a claim.

### Contractual rights under your lease

Your lease is a binding contract. Whatever it says, both parties are bound by it. This includes:

- **Rent review mechanisms** — the lease sets out how and when rent can be reviewed
- **Option rights** — if you have an option to renew, the landlord must honour it
- **Assignment and subletting rights** — what you can and can't do with the space
- **Make-good obligations** — what you must do at the end of the lease

### General contract law

As a commercial tenant, you have the same rights as any party to a contract under Australian law. Misleading conduct, unconscionable conduct, and duress are still actionable.

## Where commercial tenants are most exposed

### Make-good disputes

Make-good clauses are one of the most common sources of dispute at lease end. If your lease says you must return the space to its original condition, the landlord can present a significant bill. Without a clear written record of the space's condition at the start of the lease, disputes become expensive and hard to resolve.

**What to do:** Get a condition report at the start of the lease. Photograph everything. Negotiate the make-good clause before you sign.

### Rent review mechanisms

Commercial leases often include market rent reviews. Unlike CPI or fixed increases, a market review can result in a significant rent jump — or theoretically a decrease. If the lease doesn't clearly define the review process, disputes arise.

**What to do:** Cap rent reviews where possible. Understand exactly what the lease says before signing.

### No mandatory cooling-off period

Unlike residential leases or some retail leases, commercial leases generally have no statutory cooling-off period. Once you sign, you're bound.

## Practical steps to protect yourself

- **Use a commercial solicitor** — not a general practitioner, a commercial property specialist
- **Negotiate before you sign** — once the lease is executed, your leverage disappears
- **Use a tenant rep** — a tenant representative reviews lease terms commercially, not just legally
- **Document the condition of the space** — before you move in and when you move out
- **Understand your options** — your option to renew is only exercisable within a set window, usually specified in the lease

## The bottom line

As a commercial tenant in NSW, the lease is your primary protection. That's why getting the lease right before you sign is critical. A poorly negotiated lease can expose you to significant costs and risks over the term.

If you're about to sign a commercial lease or renew one, talk to us before you commit. We can't replace your solicitor — but we can make sure the commercial terms are fair before the lawyers get involved.
